one_liner: A 24-year-old previously healthy man presents with 6 weeks of a painless enlarging neck mass and drenching night sweats. He has lost 15 pounds without trying.

stage2.hpi_extension: The neck mass was first noticed about 6 weeks ago and has progressively enlarged. It is nontender and firm. He reports drenching night sweats requiring sheet changes, unintentional 15-lb weight loss (>10% of body weight), and recurrent low-grade fevers (Pel-Ebstein pattern noted on his home thermometer). He also reports generalized pruritus and curiously describes pain in the enlarged cervical nodes after drinking alcohol. No cough, dyspnea, or dysphagia.
stage2.social_history: Graduate student. Non-smoker, occasional beer. No IVDU.
stage2.physical_exam: General: thin young man. HEENT: firm, rubbery, nontender 4 cm left supraclavicular lymph node; additional enlarged left cervical nodes. Lungs: clear. Cardiac: regular. Abdomen: soft, no hepatosplenomegaly. No axillary or inguinal lymphadenopathy. Skin: excoriations from scratching; no rash.

lab_results.result: WBC 11.2k with mild eosinophilia, Hgb 12.8 g/dL, platelets 280k. ESR markedly elevated. LDH mildly elevated. No circulating abnormal cells.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Eosinophilia and elevated ESR/LDH are common in Hodgkin lymphoma. LDH and ESR are used for prognostic scoring.

lab_results.result: Excisional biopsy of the cervical lymph node: effacement of nodal architecture with a polymorphous reactive infiltrate (lymphocytes, eosinophils, plasma cells, histiocytes) and scattered large binucleate cells with mirror-image nuclei and prominent eosinophilic inclusion-like nucleoli — classic 'owl-eye' Reed-Sternberg cells. Immunohistochemistry: Reed-Sternberg cells are CD15+ and CD30+, CD20−, CD45−. Nodular sclerosing subtype features broad collagen bands.
lab_results.clinical_significance: CD15+ CD30+ Reed-Sternberg cells in an appropriate inflammatory background are pathognomonic for classical Hodgkin lymphoma. Nodular sclerosing is the most common subtype, especially in young adults.

lab_results.result: PET/CT for staging: FDG-avid left cervical, supraclavicular, and mediastinal lymphadenopathy with a large anterior mediastinal mass. No subdiaphragmatic disease. No splenic or hepatic involvement.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Disease limited to nodal groups above the diaphragm demonstrates the characteristic contiguous spread of Hodgkin lymphoma and defines early-stage disease. Presence of B symptoms upstages the patient.

stage5.final_clue: He is diagnosed with nodular sclerosing classical Hodgkin lymphoma, stage IIB, and initiated on ABVD chemotherapy (doxorubicin, bleomycin, vinblastine, dacarbazine) with excellent anticipated cure rate.

debrief.classic_presentation: Hodgkin lymphoma has a bimodal age distribution, with peaks in young adults (~20s) and adults >55. Classic presentation: a young patient with painless, non-tender, rubbery cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy; B symptoms (fever >38°C, drenching night sweats, unintentional weight loss >10% in 6 months); pruritus; and the rare but pathognomonic finding of lymph node pain after alcohol ingestion. Pel-Ebstein (cyclic) fever is a classic teaching buzzword. Nodular sclerosing is the most common subtype, especially in young women.
debrief.pathophysiology: Hodgkin lymphoma is a B-cell neoplasm defined by the presence of Reed-Sternberg (RS) cells — large binucleate (or multinucleate) cells with prominent eosinophilic nucleoli giving an 'owl eye' appearance. RS cells are CD15+ and CD30+ but CD20− and CD45−, distinguishing them from most other B-cell lymphomas. Despite being the malignant cells, RS cells make up a minority of the tumor; most of the mass is a reactive inflammatory infiltrate recruited by cytokines. EBV is associated with some subtypes (especially mixed cellularity). Disease spreads in a predictable contiguous manner from one nodal group to adjacent ones.
debrief.treatment_overview: Early-stage favorable disease is treated with a short course of ABVD chemotherapy (doxorubicin, bleomycin, vinblastine, dacarbazine) ± involved-site radiation. Advanced disease receives full ABVD or dose-escalated regimens. Hodgkin lymphoma is highly curable — overall cure rates exceed 80–90% for early-stage disease. Late complications of therapy include secondary malignancies (breast cancer, AML), cardiomyopathy (doxorubicin), and pulmonary fibrosis (bleomycin).
high_yield_pearls: Hodgkin lymphoma = Reed-Sternberg cells (CD15+ CD30+, 'owl eyes'), bimodal age, contiguous spread, and usually curable.
B symptoms = fever >38°C, drenching night sweats, unintentional weight loss >10% in 6 months (upstages disease).
Nodular sclerosing is the most common subtype; lymphocyte-rich has the best prognosis; lymphocyte-depleted has the worst.
Classic buzzwords: pruritus, Pel-Ebstein fevers, lymph node pain after alcohol intake.
Hodgkin vs non-Hodgkin: Hodgkin is contiguous nodal spread and B-symptom heavy; NHL is more common, more extranodal, and less orderly.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Hematology and Oncology chapter, 'Lymphomas — Hodgkin lymphoma' section.
